title: practical english usage - Part 78
content:
We're all excited about his arrival. (     ... about his arriving) usually preferred.

When  there is a noun which  has a similar meaning to an -ing form, the noun is 6 nouns     and   -ing    forms I didn't think it worth complaining about the meal.

It's no use his/him apologising -I will never forgive him.

Is it any use expecting them to be on time?

It's no good talking to him  - he never listens.

This is common with any/no good, any/no use and (not) worth (►  634).

I thought it pointless starting before eight o'clock.

It's nice being with you.

We   can use it as a preparatory subject or object for an -ing form (►  268-269).

5  It's  nice     being       with     you.

grammar •  96  participles: -ing and -ed forms used like adjectives vanished civilisations increased activity              a grown-up daughter swollen ankles developed countries          a collapsed building        faded colours advanced students            a retired general               an escaped prisoner ɑ  fallen leaf (= a leaf i       has fallen) nouns.

Some past participles can be used as adjectives with active meanings before 4 active     past    participles:       a  fallen      leaf;    advanced          students (sor .. . nnvconfusing.) She explained everything but I was still very confused.

- He’s a very confusing writer.

The kids get excited when Granny comes, (     ... The kids get exciting-. ..) - Granny takes the kids to exciting places.

I didn’t enjoy the party because I was bored, (     .. . bvtcntse-Lwas Inning.) - It was a terribly boring party.

I was very interested in the lesson, (     ! was wy-interesting infhedeswn.) - The lesson was really interesting.

have passive meanings: they show how  people are made to feel. Compare: describe things that interest people, bore them and so on. Interested, bored, etc example, interesting, boring, exciting and confusing have active meanings: they convenience. These adjectives, too, may have an active/passive contrast. For adjectives and participles is not always clear). They  are discussed here for Some adjectives look like present or past participles (and the difference between 3 participles        and   adjectives:       interested           and   interesting,           etc The house looked abandoned. (= ... as if it had been abandoned.) a broken heart (= a heart that has been broken) Most past participles have passive meanings when they are used like adjectives.

a meat-eating animal (= an animal that eats meat) falling leaves (= leaves that fall) active verbs.

When -ing forms are used like adjectives, they have similar meanings to 2 active     and   passive ►    Section  1).

(The same terms are used when these forms are used as parts of verb tenses, These cakes are beautifully cooked. Daniel will be surprised to see you.

I was taken to hospital with a broken arm.

The house is going to be full of screaming children.

We haven’t got a single working computer in the office.

We were nearly hit by falling rocks.

But note that both forms can be used for the past, present or future.

participles’ . Forms like broken, sung, opened, started are called ’ past participles*.

When  -ing forms are used in similar ways to adjectives, they are called ‘ present 1 names like   adjectives 96 participles:       -ing    and   -ed   forms     used Infinitives,      -ing    forms     and   Past   Participles       Section 8 grammar •  96  participles: -ing and -ed forms used like adjectives the solution adopted (= the solution that is/was chosen) biological parents) - an adopted child (= a child who  is brought up by people who  are not his/her the people involved (= the same as the people concerned) - an involved explanation (= a complicated explanation) the people concerned (= the people who  are/were affected) - a concerned expression (= a worried expression) A  few participles change their meaning according to their position. Compare: 7 differences        of  meaning Those selected will begin training on Monday.

Most of those questioned refused to answer.
